1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale:

  2. Methods:

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 signifies … Values decreasing toward zero reflect…

  4. Results and development needs:

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

Bakgrunn og begrunnelse:

Metoder:

Tolkning:

Resultater og utviklingsbehov:

2. About the underlying data

The old forest share of area is recorded from plots in the Norwegian National Forest Inventory (NFI, Landsskogtakseringen). There are about 12 000 NFI plots in forest ecosystems, distributed on a regular grid, with varying grid density for forest below/above the coniferous forest line and in Finnmark county. The indicator value is an unbiased area-weighted estimate of the old forest share of area for NFI plots in any selected region. All NFI plots are sampled over a 5-year period, with a representative sampling scheme every year, allowing unbiased estimates from data for each year. For this indicator data from each full inventory cycle of 5 years are used, starting in 2010, meaning that results reported for 2024 are based on data from the years 2020-2024. The data are available upon request from NFI.

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Data are available from 2010, when the the old forest share of area was first recorded based on current methods. Annual estimates are possible due to representative sampling every year. For this indicator all data from each full inventory cycle of 5 years are used, starting in 2010. The data are representative for all forest land of Norway. Spatial resolution should be limited to the resolution applied to reference values, that is counties or sub-counties for scaling and regions for aggregated reporting.

2.2 Sampling design

2.3 Original units

Each forest inventory plot carries a binary presence variable bgs (0/1): whether biologically old forest occurs on that plot, and au_areal is the forest area on each plot (ha). At county (fylke), region, or national level, the non-scaled share of old forest is the ratio of total old forest area on plots, divided by total forest area on all forest plots in the unit.

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

The data used for this indicator have been made available by the Norwegian National Forest Inventory under a specific data use agreement of 2026-05-29, between NIBIO and the specified users. Further use of these data requires a separate data user agreement with NIBIO.

2.5 Additional comments about the dataset

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

C1 - Landscape and Seascape Characteristics

3.1.1 Other condition typologies

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

3.4 Impact factors

3.5 Spatial aggregation

The spatial aggregation pathway is Aggregate (NFI plots to county/sub-county) – [Scale – Truncate] – Aggregate (geographic region) – Aggregate (national level). For each reporting period, non-scaled values for county (fylke), region, and Norway are obtained by pooling all forest plots in the unit: the total forest area on plots where old forest is present, divided by the total forest area on all forest plots in that unit. This is done for counties or sub-counties, regions, and at national level. Scaling and truncation are applied only after these non-scaled county or sub-county aggregates have been calculated: each county or sub-county is scaled against its own lower reference level X0 (0% share) and upper reference level X100 using a linear scaling function, and values above 1 are truncated to 1 at that step. Regional and national scaled point estimates are area-weighted means of the already scaled county or sub-county values, using each county or sub-county’s represented area as weight. Non-scaled regional and national point estimates are calculated directly from all plots within each region or the whole country.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition is considered to be intact forest ecosystems where effects of human activities are clearly subordinate to those of natural ecosystem processes and disturbances.

4.2 Reference levels

The lower reference level X0 is set to 0% old forest share of area and the upper reference level X100 is set separately for each county or sub-county. X0 denotes the worst possible condition and X100 denotes the best possible condition on the original share of area scale. Between X0 and X100, indicator values are scaled linearly to a 0-1 indicator scale. Scaled values above 1 are truncated to 1 when old forest share of area exceeds the county-specific X100. The county and sub-county X100 values used in the analyses are listed in Section 9.4. X0 is 0 for all units.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels are defined at county or sub-county resolution. Scaling is therefore applied to area-weighted non-scaled indicator values that have first been aggregated to the matching county or sub-county unit. Regional and national reporting uses area-weighted aggregation of these scaled county or sub-county values rather than one common X100 for the aggregated non-scaled value. The indicator should not be interpreted at finer spatial resolution than the county or sub-county reference levels allow.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated). Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of counties, so they inherit both model uncertainty at county level and any mismatch between county boundaries and ecological variation within regions.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The uncertainties reported in Section 10 are inferential and refer to uncertainty around the point estimates, not to the full spatial variation in old forest share of area among forest plots. For each reporting period, non-scaled indicator values are bootstrapped by resampling forest plots with replacement within the relevant geographic unit and recomputing the forest-area share. This is done separately for counties or sub-counties, regions, and the national level.

For scaled county or sub-county values, bootstrap draws are transformed with the same county-specific reference levels as the point estimates (truncation at scaling). For scaled regional and national values, uncertainty is propagated from the scaled county bootstrap draws. The resulting distributions are summarised with median and quantile intervals.

6. References

7. Import and prepare datasets

The old forest area share is built from each forest inventory plot: binary presence bgs (biologically old forest, 0/1). For this indicator data from each full 5-year inventory cycle, starting in 2010, are used.

Load data 
# This export wraps each physical line in an extra pair of double quotes, so a
# single read_csv() sees one column spanning the whole file. Read once as one
# character column per line (readr unwraps the outer quotes), then parse the
# inner CSV text.
lsk_inner_csv <- read_csv(
  here("data/LSK_data_okologisk_tilstand_2026.csv"),
  col_names = FALSE,
  show_col_types = FALSE,
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8"),
  col_types = cols(.default = col_character())
) |>
  pull(1) |>
  paste(collapse = "\n")

lsk_data_raw <- read_csv(
  I(lsk_inner_csv),
  show_col_types = FALSE,
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")
) |>
  clean_names()

8. Spatial units

Aggregated indicator values should not be applied for spatial units finer than those that allow reliable estimates, e.g., regions.

9. Analyses

9.1 Prepare data and create derived variables

This section prepares the NFI plot data for calculating the indicator.

First we define the 5-year reporting periods that match the temporal structure of the indicator.

Define reporting periods 
reporting_periods <- list(
  "2010-2014" = 2010:2014,
  "2015-2019" = 2015:2019,
  "2020-2024" = 2020:2024
)

The analysis includes both productive and unproductive forest plots (arealtype 1 and 12). The LSK export contains the binary variable bgs (0/1) for biologically old forest on each forest plot. bgs is the plot-level indicator on forest area (au_areal, ha): 1 = old forest present on the plot, 0 = not. We store the per-plot numerator as indicator_continuous_weighted = bgs * au_areal; area-weighted aggregation to fylke, region, and national units is in Section 9.2.

Create derived variables 
lsk_data <- lsk_data_raw |>
  dplyr::filter(arealtype %in% c(1, 12)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    fylke_name = region_fylke |>
      stringr::str_remove("^\\d+-\\d+\\s+") |>
      stringr::str_remove("^\\d+\\s*") |>
      stringr::str_squish(),
    county_part = assign_bbca_county_part(fylke_name),
    fylke_name_clean = stringi::stri_trans_general(
      stringr::str_squish(
        tolower(
          stringr::str_replace_all(
            normalize_county_parts(fylke_name),
            "[0-9.-]",
            " "
          )
        )
      ),
      "Latin-ASCII"
    ),
    region_code = assign_region_code(fylke_name_clean),
    region_name = assign_region_name(region_code),
    bgs = as.numeric(bgs),
    indicator_continuous_weighted = bgs * au_areal
  )

Finally, we retain only the columns required for subsequent aggregation and reporting.

Select downstream fields 
lsk_data <- lsk_data |>
  dplyr::select(
    sesong,
    au_areal,
    arealtype,
    region_code,
    region_name,
    county_part,
    fylke_name,
    fylke_name_clean,
    bgs,
    indicator_continuous_weighted
  )

9.2 Calculate non-scaled area-weighted indicator values

For each reporting period, we calculate non-scaled indicator values separately for counties or sub-counties, regions, and the whole country. Within each geographic unit, the share of inventoried forest area on plots with old forest present is

\[\frac{\sum_i (\mathrm{bgs}_i \cdot a_i)}{\sum_i a_i}\]

where \(a_i\) is plot forest area (au_areal, ha).

Calculate non-scaled values 
bgsk_results <- purrr::imap(
  reporting_periods,
  ~ calculate_period_indicators(lsk_data, .y, .x)
) |>
  purrr::compact()

bgsk_results_county_all_periods   <- purrr::map_dfr(bgsk_results, "county")
bgsk_results_region_all_periods   <- purrr::map_dfr(bgsk_results, "region")
bgsk_results_national_all_periods <- purrr::map_dfr(bgsk_results, "national")

9.3 Bootstrap draws per county

For each county and period, NFI plots are resampled with replacement 1000 times. Each replicate computes the area-weighted non-scaled share of old forest, giving a distribution of 1000 draws per county.

Bootstrap draws per county (1000 per county per period) 
county_draws_long <- purrr::imap_dfr(bgsk_results, function(period_result, period_name) {
  county_meta <- period_result$county |>
    dplyr::select(fylke_name, county_part, total_area, region_code)

  purrr::imap_dfr(
    split(period_result$data, period_result$data$fylke_name),
    function(county_data, county_name) {
      boot <- bootstrap_indicator(county_data, n_bootstrap = 1000)
      dplyr::tibble(
        period           = period_name,
        fylke_name       = county_name,
        sim_id           = seq_along(boot$draws),
        non_scaled_value = boot$draws
      )
    }
  ) |>
    dplyr::left_join(county_meta, by = "fylke_name")
})

The table below (Table 2) summarises non-scaled bootstrap draws for all reporting periods: the median area-weighted old forest share per county or sub-county. Use the search box and page controls to browse rows.

9.4 Scale indicator values and aggregate to regional and national levels

County or sub-county reference levels are listed below.

Reference values by county or sub-county 
bgsk_reference_values <- dplyr::tibble(
  county_part = c(
    "Øs", "OA", "HeS", "HeN", "OpS", "OpN", "BuS", "BuV", "Ve",
    "TeS", "TeN", "AA", "VA", "Ro", "Ho", "SF", "MR", "ST", "NT",
    "NoS", "NoN", "Tr", "Fi"
  ),
  x100_pct = c(
    50, 50, 50, 45, 50, 45, 50, 45, 50, 50, 45, 45, 45, 40, 40,
    40, 40, 45, 45, 45, 30, 30, 30
  )
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(x100 = x100_pct / 100)

x0 <- 0
county_reference <- bgsk_reference_values |>
  dplyr::select(county_part, x100)

knitr::kable(bgsk_reference_values |> dplyr::select(county_part, `X100 (%)` = x100_pct))
county_part X100 (%)
Øs 50
OA 50
HeS 50
HeN 45
OpS 50
OpN 45
BuS 50
BuV 45
Ve 50
TeS 50
TeN 45
AA 45
VA 45
Ro 40
Ho 40
SF 40
MR 40
ST 45
NT 45
NoS 45
NoN 30
Tr 30
Fi 30

Each county’s 1000 non-scaled draws from section 9.3 are scaled with ecTools::ec_normalise() (linear, X0 = 0, county-specific X100; truncation at scaling). Figure 1 shows the mapping for each sub-county.

Scale county draws; derive county uncertainty and feed ec_upscale 
county_draws_scaled <- county_draws_long |>
  dplyr::inner_join(county_reference, by = "county_part") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    scaled_value = ec_normalise(
      non_scaled_value,
      x0 = x0,
      x100 = x100,
      fun = "linear"
    ),
    nation = "National"
  )

county_scaled_with_uncertainty <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, fylke_name, county_part, total_area, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(scaled_value),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.025)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.25)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.75)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    bgsk_results_county_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, fylke_name, n_plots),
    by = c("period", "fylke_name")
  )
Normalisation diagnostic figure 
norm_plot_data <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::group_by(county_part) |>
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 100) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

bgsk_norm_plot <- ec_norm_plot(norm_plot_data$non_scaled_value, norm_plot_data$scaled_value) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ norm_plot_data$county_part, scales = "free_x") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Old forest share (proportion of forest area)",
    y = "Scaled indicator value"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme(axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9))
bgsk_norm_plot$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 3

suppressMessages(
  ggsave(
    filename = here::here("img/bgsk_norm_plot.png"),
    plot = bgsk_norm_plot,
    scale = 1.5
  )
)

bgsk_norm_plot

The scaled draws are then passed to ecTools::ec_upscale(), which propagates uncertainty through the spatial hierarchy. For each of 1000 Monte Carlo iterations, one draw is sampled per county and the area-weighted mean gives one regional value; a second ec_upscale() call repeats this from regions to national level.

ec_upscale: county to region (compute once, save RDS) 
bgsk_mc_cache_deps <- c(
  here::here("data/LSK_data_okologisk_tilstand_2026.csv"),
  here::here("R/functions.R"),
  here::here("R/NO_BGSK_001.qmd")
)
bgsk_mc_rds_regional <- here::here("data/bgsk_mc_regional.rds")

if (
  file.exists(bgsk_mc_rds_regional) &&
  file.mtime(bgsk_mc_rds_regional) >= max(file.mtime(bgsk_mc_cache_deps), na.rm = TRUE)
) {
  bgsk_mc_regional <- readRDS(bgsk_mc_rds_regional)
} else {
  bgsk_mc_regional <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
    data        = county_draws_scaled,
    variable    = scaled_value,
    weight      = total_area,
    start_units = fylke_name,
    end_units   = region_code,
    year        = period,
    end_units_name = "region_code",
    n = 1000
  )
  saveRDS(bgsk_mc_regional, bgsk_mc_rds_regional)
}
ec_upscale: region to national (compute once, save RDS) 
region_area_weights <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::distinct(period, fylke_name, total_area, region_code) |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(region_area = sum(total_area), .groups = "drop")

bgsk_mc_regional_for_national <- bgsk_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::left_join(region_area_weights, by = c("period", "region_code")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(nation = "National")

bgsk_mc_rds_national <- here::here("data/bgsk_mc_national.rds")

if (
  file.exists(bgsk_mc_rds_national) &&
  file.mtime(bgsk_mc_rds_national) >= max(
    c(file.mtime(bgsk_mc_cache_deps), file.mtime(bgsk_mc_rds_regional)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
) {
  bgsk_mc_national <- readRDS(bgsk_mc_rds_national)
} else {
  bgsk_mc_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
    data        = bgsk_mc_regional_for_national,
    variable    = sampled_mean,
    weight      = region_area,
    start_units = region_code,
    end_units   = nation,
    year        = period,
    end_units_name = "nation",
    n = 1000
  )
  saveRDS(bgsk_mc_national, bgsk_mc_rds_national)
}
Combine regional and national MC draws; build summary tables 
bgsk_mc_combined <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  bgsk_mc_regional |> dplyr::select(period, region = region_code, sampled_mean),
  bgsk_mc_national |> dplyr::select(period, region = nation, sampled_mean)
)

county_scaled_all_periods <- bgsk_results_county_all_periods |>
  dplyr::left_join(county_reference, by = "county_part") |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(x100)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    scaled_value = ec_normalise(indicator_value, x0 = x0, x100 = x100, fun = "linear")
  )

region_scaled_agg_all_periods <- bgsk_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(scaled_value = median(sampled_mean), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = assign_region_name(region_code))

national_scaled_agg_all_periods <- bgsk_mc_national |>
  dplyr::group_by(period) |>
  dplyr::summarise(scaled_value = median(sampled_mean), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(region = "National")

region_scaled_agg_with_uncertainty <- bgsk_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.25)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.75)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = assign_region_name(region_code)) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    bgsk_results_region_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, region_code, total_area, n_plots),
    by = c("period", "region_code")
  )

national_scaled_agg_with_uncertainty <- bgsk_mc_national |>
  dplyr::group_by(period) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.25)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.75)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region = "National") |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    bgsk_results_national_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, total_area, n_plots),
    by = "period"
  )

truncation_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  county_scaled_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      bgsk_reference_values |> dplyr::select(county_part, reference_value_pct = x100_pct),
      by = "county_part"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "County",
      exceeds_threshold = indicator_value > x100,
      original_value_pct = round(indicator_value * 100, 2),
      region_code = NA_character_,
      region_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    ),
  region_scaled_agg_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      bgsk_results_region_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, region_code, indicator_value),
      by = c("period", "region_code")
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "Region",
      exceeds_threshold = scaled_value >= 1,
      original_value_pct = round(indicator_value * 100, 2),
      reference_value_pct = NA_real_,
      fylke_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    ),
  national_scaled_agg_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      bgsk_results_national_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, indicator_value),
      by = "period"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "National",
      exceeds_threshold = scaled_value >= 1,
      original_value_pct = round(indicator_value * 100, 2),
      reference_value_pct = NA_real_,
      region_code = NA_character_,
      region_name = "National",
      fylke_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    )
)

truncation_counts <- truncation_summary |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, level) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_total = dplyr::n(),
    n_exceeding_reference = sum(exceeds_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_exceeding_reference = round(100 * n_exceeding_reference / n_total, 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

truncated_values <- truncation_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(exceeds_threshold) |>
  dplyr::arrange(period, level, dplyr::desc(original_value_pct))

At county or sub-county level, truncation is evaluated by comparing the non-scaled point estimate to the matching X100. At regional and national level, the tables report cases where the ec_upscale() median equals 1. The following tables summarise how often truncation occurred in each period and list the affected units.

Period Level Total Exceeding reference % Exceeding reference
2010-2014 County 23 0 0.0
2010-2014 National 1 0 0.0
2010-2014 Region 5 0 0.0
2015-2019 County 23 0 0.0
2015-2019 National 1 0 0.0
2015-2019 Region 5 0 0.0
2020-2024 County 23 0 0.0
2020-2024 National 1 0 0.0
2020-2024 Region 5 0 0.0 

No values exceeded their reference and required truncation.

The time series below (Figure 2) summarises regional and national values.

Time series of indicator values by region and period 
bgsk_region_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Øst", "2" = "Sør", "3" = "Vest",
  "4" = "Midt", "5" = "Nord", "National" = "National"
)
bgsk_region_levels <- c("National", "Midt", "Nord", "Øst", "Sør", "Vest")
bgsk_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(bgsk_region_levels)),
  bgsk_region_levels
)

bgsk_plot_data <- bgsk_mc_combined |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    region_name = dplyr::recode(region, !!!bgsk_region_labels),
    series_type = dplyr::if_else(region == "National", "National", "Region")
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_name, series_type) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = factor(region_name, levels = bgsk_region_levels))

ggplot2::ggplot(
  bgsk_plot_data,
  ggplot2::aes(x = period, y = median, colour = region_name, group = region_name)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(
    ggplot2::aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_pointrange(
    ggplot2::aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45,
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = bgsk_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_shape_manual(values = c(National = 19, Region = 17), name = NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_linewidth_manual(values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55), guide = "none") +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.box         = "horizontal",
    panel.grid.minor   = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = ggplot2::element_blank()
  ) +
  ggplot2::guides(
    colour = ggplot2::guide_legend(
      nrow = 2,
      override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)
    ),
    shape  = ggplot2::guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")

10. Results

The following tables present scaled indicator results at county or sub-county, regional, and national scales for each reporting period. Tables report the median as the indicator value, with an approximate 95% interval (2.5% and 97.5% quantiles) and an approximate interquartile range (25% and 75% quantiles).

10.1 County-level results (scaled with uncertainty)

Use the search box and page controls in Table 3 to browse county rows across all reporting periods.

10.2 Regional results (scaled with uncertainty)

10.3 National results (scaled with uncertainty)

11. Export file

Regional and national Monte Carlo draws are exported in one table (bgsk_mc_combined: period, region, sampled_mean).

Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws 
write.csv(
  bgsk_mc_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_BGSK_001.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

MeRIT attribution

This part supplements the CRediT statement with more granularity for method section.

Study design The study was designed by [AB, CD].

Data collection / fieldwork Field data were collected by [AB, EF] between [years]. Sampling protocol was developed by [CD].

Laboratory procedures DNA extraction was performed by [GH]. Sequencing was conducted by [IJ].

Data processing Raw data were curated and cleaned by [EF]. Code for preprocessing was written by [KL].

Statistical analysis All analyses were conducted by [CD] using [software], with verification by [AB].

Visualization Figures were produced by [KL], with input from [CD].

Reproducibility All code was reviewed and validated by [AB, CD].
